title: The Sunday Slow Morning
subtitle: A weekly ritual for setting the pace of the week from the kitchen, not the inbox
excerpt: A weekly Sunday-morning ritual that holds a slow space in the kitchen before the week starts. The week that begins from a slow Sunday is a different week from one that begins from a rushed Monday inbox.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Slow Sunday is a tradition in many slow-living households and in many religious customs around the Sabbath.

practice.whenToUse: Use on Sunday mornings as a weekly ritual. Especially good when the previous week was hard and the next week is going to be busy.

content.text: A Sunday-morning ritual that sets a slow space in the kitchen before the week starts. The week begun from a slow Sunday is a different week from one begun from a Monday inbox.
New to ritual as a practice? Read
how rituals work
first.
What you will need
An hour. Real breakfast food. The kitchen, fresh from a wipe-down. No phone.
The practice
Prepare

On Saturday evening, lay the table for breakfast. Plates, cups, a small jug for milk, a small bowl for fruit. Set the kettle so it is ready. The Sunday morning then opens to a table that is already set.
Release (repeat x3)
I am ready to release the rush that the week has put in my shoulders. I release it now. I release it now. I release it now.
Allow (repeat x3)
I am ready to allow this hour to be slow. I allow it now. I allow it now. I allow it now.
Integrate

Make the breakfast slowly. Coffee or tea, fruit, toast, eggs if you want them. Eat at the table, not at the counter. If you read, read a book. If you sit with someone, talk slowly. If you are on your own, the quiet is the company.
When the breakfast is done, the dishes go straight in the bowl. The kitchen is reset. The Sunday is allowed to go on at its own pace.
Ground it
The week begins from this kitchen, not from the inbox.
Say it once before you leave the kitchen. Carry the pace of the morning into the rest of the day.
Where this practice comes from

Original to homemade.education. The Slow Sunday is a tradition in many slow-living households and in many religious customs that mark a day of rest. The form here uses the five-part ritual shape (prepare, release, allow, integrate, ground) so the morning has a beginning and a close.
